content.text: package ships two lint rules that enforce correct hook usage at the editor and CI level. They catch violations before they reach runtime — conditional hook calls, missing effect dependencies, and hooks called from invalid locations — giving immediate, actionable feedback with the exact file and line number. Both rules should be part of every React project's ESLint configuration.

attrs.alt: The two rules of eslint-plugin-react-hooks: rules-of-hooks and exhaustive-deps

content.text: The plugin is maintained by the React team and is included in
(Next.js), and Vite's React template. In projects not using these presets, install and configure it manually:
The plugin exposes exactly two rules.
enforces the two fundamental call-site constraints: hooks only at the top level, hooks only from React functions.
enforces that every value referenced inside an effect, memo, or callback is listed in the dependency array. The recommended config sets
; strict projects often upgrade exhaustive-deps to
rule performs static analysis of call expressions. It identifies hook calls by checking whether the callee name starts with
(case-sensitive) and whether the call appears inside a React component or custom hook. It understands JSX context to determine when a function is a component, and the
prefix convention to identify custom hooks. The analysis is flow-insensitive — it considers code structure, not runtime values.

content.text: rule analyzes the dependency arrays of
. It walks the callback's AST and collects every free variable reference (values from outer scope). It then compares this set against the declared dependency array and reports any mismatch.

is a variable used in the callback but absent from the array. This creates a stale closure: the effect captures the value at the time the effect was created, not the latest value. Missing deps are the most common source of subtle bugs in effects — the code appears to work but uses outdated data.

is a value listed in the array but not actually used inside the callback. Extra deps are not bugs — they cause unnecessary effect re-runs but produce correct behavior. The linter warns on them because they signal either dead code in the deps array or a misunderstanding of what the effect does.
Stable Values and the Deps Array
The rule understands that certain values are guaranteed stable across renders and excludes them from the "missing deps" report:
value). These are safe to omit because React guarantees their identity never changes. Including them is harmless but adds noise.
When the linter reports a missing dependency, there are three responses:

content: : the simplest fix. If the effect should re-run when the value changes, add it to the array. This is correct in the majority of cases.
: if the effect does not actually need to re-run when the value changes (e.g., you only need the initial value), use a ref to hold the value without subscribing to its changes:
and read
inside the effect.
: when an effect only reads state to compute its next value, switch to the updater form of
) — this removes the state variable from the deps because the effect no longer reads it directly.

content.text: When suppressing a rule with a disable comment, always include a reason inline so future readers understand the intent:
If the project uses ESLint's flat config format (
), the configuration differs from the legacy
Lint rules are only as good as their CI enforcement. Running ESLint in CI catches violations that developers might dismiss in their editor. The
flag causes ESLint to exit with a non-zero code if any
-level rule fires, which is useful when you want to treat

# Fail the build if any lint error OR warning fires
npx eslint src/ --max-warnings 0

# Typical CI script (package.json)
# "lint": "eslint src/ --max-warnings 0"
rule provides an autofix for the most common case — adding missing dependencies. Running

on a file with missing deps inserts them automatically. However, the autofix should always be reviewed: adding a missing dep sometimes exposes a loop (the dep changes on every render because it is an inline object or function), which requires restructuring the code rather than just adding to the array.

# Autofix missing deps in a single file
npx eslint --fix src/components/UserCard.jsx

# Review the diff before committing — autofix may need adjustments
git diff src/components/UserCard.jsx
The plugin supports configuration for custom hooks that wrap the built-in hooks with dependency arrays. If you write a
that accepts a callback and deps, you can register it so the linter applies
analysis to your hook's arguments too:
Hook in condition / loop / non-React fn / after early return
Move hook to top level of component or custom hook
Missing dep (stale closure) or extra dep (unnecessary re-run)
Add missing dep, restructure, or use updater form
Dependency Loops: When Adding Deps Creates Infinite Re-renders
is when adding a missing dependency creates an infinite update loop. This happens when the dependency is a new object or function on every render:
The root cause is not the dep array — it is that the value is not stable. The fix is to stabilize the value with
, move it outside the component if it is a constant, or restructure the effect so it does not depend on the unstable value.
Object and array literals created inline during render are

referentially equal to the object or array from the previous render, even if their contents are identical. This means adding them to a deps array immediately triggers the effect on every render. The correct approach is to depend on the
inside the object rather than the object itself:
Functions defined inline during render are also new references on every render. An effect that depends on an inline callback will re-run on every render. The solution is
to stabilize the function reference, or restructuring so the effect does not depend on the function at all.
React Compiler (the Babel/SWC transform introduced with React 19) automatically memoizes components and values to minimize re-renders. When the compiler is active, it injects the equivalent of

enforcement becomes less critical for preventing stale closures — the compiler tracks dependencies itself. The rule still runs but the compiler's output may suppress some warnings that would otherwise fire in hand-written code.
With React Compiler active, manual exhaustive-deps compliance is less critical — but rules-of-hooks remains fully enforced regardless. Never disable rules-of-hooks when using the compiler.
prevents hooks from being called in invalid positions (conditions, loops, non-React functions), and

catches stale closures by ensuring every referenced value appears in the dependency array. Together they turn runtime hook violations into editor-time errors, making the rules of hooks practically enforceable without relying on developer memory.
